It's a React-based Single Page Application (SPA) that runs in the browser and connects to a running Mastra server . There are two primary ways of deploying Studio: Mastra Cloud hosts Studio for you and allows you to share access with your team via link You can self-host Studio on your own infrastructure, either alongside your Mastra server or separately as a standalone SPA On this page you'll learn how to deploy Studio on your own infrastructure. As the finer details of deployment can vary widely based on your needs and setup, we'll cover the general principles and options available to you. Fundamentals Direct link to Fundamentals The easiest way to run Studio is the mastra studio command. Whereas mastra dev runs both Studio and an API for development purposes, the purpose of mastra studio is to serve a standalone, static UI that connects to an already-running Mastra server. This allows you to deploy Studio on its own, separate from the Mastra server, if desired. Open a terminal and install the mastra CLI globally: npm pnpm Yarn Bun npm install -g mastra pnpm add -g mastra yarn global add mastra bun add --global mastra Run the mastra studio command: mastra studio Open localhost:3000 in your browser to see the Studio UI. By default, it will attempt to connect to a Mastra server running at http://localhost:4111 . If it doesn't find one there, you'll see a form where you can enter your Mastra instance URL and API prefix. If you're hosting Studio under a subpath (for example /agents behind Nginx), set MASTRA_STUDIO_BASE_PATH before starting Studio: MASTRA_STUDIO_BASE_PATH = /agents mastra studio This updates the HTML base URL and static asset routing so the standalone Studio works correctly under that subpath. The command uses Node's built-in http module and serve-handler to serve the static files. Running a server Direct link to Running a server Running mastra studio as a long-running process is no different from running any other Node.js service. All the best practices, tools, and options for deployment apply here as well. You can use process managers like PM2, use Docker, or cloud services that support Node.js applications. You'll need to ensure CORS is configured correctly and errors are monitored, just like with any web service. warning Once Studio is connected to your Mastra server, it has full access to your agents, workflows, and tools. Be sure to secure it properly in production (e.g. behind authentication, VPN, etc.) to prevent unauthorized access. Alongside your API Direct link to Alongside your API Alternatively, you can serve Studio alongside your Mastra server . This way you only have one service to deploy and manage. To do this, run mastra build with the --studio flag: mastra build --studio It'll create a .mastra/output/studio folder with the built Studio assets. By defining the MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH environment variable, the Mastra server can serve the Studio UI, too. MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH = .mastra/output/studio node .mastra/output/index.mjs Using a CDN Direct link to Using a CDN Currently, you can't directly deploy the built Studio assets to a CDN, as the UI relies on some dynamic configuration. With a bit of extra setup, you can create a standalone SPA out of the built assets and deploy it to any static hosting service like Netlify, Vercel, etc. Follow the example below to create a SPA using Vite. This guide covers monorepo-specific considerations. For the core build and deployment steps, see Deploy a Mastra Server . Supported monorepos Direct link to Supported monorepos Mastra works with: npm workspaces pnpm workspaces Yarn workspaces Turborepo Known limitations: Bun workspaces - partial support; known issues Nx - You can use Nx's supported dependency strategies but you need to have package.json files inside your workspace packages Example structure Direct link to Example structure In this example, the Mastra application is located at apps/api : apps/ ├── api/ │ ├── src/ │ │ └── mastra/ │ │ ├── agents/ │ │ ├── tools/ │ │ ├── workflows/ │ │ └── index.ts │ ├── package.json │ └── tsconfig.json └── web/ packages/ ├── ui/ └── utils/ package.json Building from a monorepo Direct link to Building from a monorepo Use your monorepo tool to run the build command from the correct package. There's no need for special flags. Examples: npm pnpm yarn Turborepo npm run build --workspace = apps/api pnpm --filter api run build yarn workspace api build turbo run build --filter = api Your package's build script should run mastra build : apps/api/package.json { \"scripts\" : { \"build\" : \"mastra build\" } } Workspace packages Direct link to Workspace packages When your Mastra application imports from other workspace packages, Mastra handles this automatically: If the package is pre-compiled (e.g., built with tsc or tsdown ), Mastra imports the compiled JavaScript If the package contains uncompiled TypeScript, Mastra transpiles it during the build For most setups, this works without configuration. If you encounter issues with workspace package imports, add the package to transpilePackages : src/mastra/index.ts export const mastra = new Mastra ( { bundler : { transpilePackages : [ '@my-org/utils' ] , } , } ) Environment variables Direct link to Environment variables Store .env files in the Mastra application directory (e.g., apps/api/.env ), not the monorepo root. Deployment configuration Direct link to Deployment configuration When deploying to cloud providers, ensure the correct package is selected as the deploy target. Selecting the monorepo root instead of the application directory (e.g., apps/api ) is a common mistake. Most providers let you specify the root directory in their dashboard or configuration file. Mastra Cloud Direct link to Mastra Cloud The image below shows how to select apps/api as the project root when deploying to Mastra Cloud . While the interface may differ between providers, the configuration remains the same. Follow the instructions below to ensure your Mastra integration deploys correctly. warning If you're deploying to a cloud provider, remove any usage of LibSQLStore from your Mastra configuration. LibSQLStore requires filesystem access and is not compatible with serverless platforms. Integration guides: With Next.js With Astro With Next.js on Vercel Direct link to With Next.js on Vercel If you've integrated Mastra with Next.js by following our guide and plan to deploy to Vercel, add serverExternalPackages: [\"@mastra/*\"] to your next.config.ts : next.config.ts import type { NextConfig } from 'next' const nextConfig : NextConfig = { serverExternalPackages : [ '@mastra/*' ] , } export default nextConfig With Astro on Vercel Direct link to With Astro on Vercel If you've integrated Mastra with Astro by following our guide and plan to deploy to Vercel, add the Vercel adapter and server output to your astro.config.mjs : astro.config.mjs import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config' import vercel from '@astrojs/vercel' export default defineConfig ( { adapter : vercel ( ) , output : 'server' , } ) With Astro on Netlify Direct link to With Astro on Netlify If you've integrated Mastra with Astro by following our guide and plan to deploy to Netlify, add the Netlify adapter and server output to your astro.config.mjs : astro.config.mjs import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config' import netlify from '@astrojs/netlify' export default defineConfig ( { adapter : netlify ( ) , It includes everything you need to go from early prototypes to production-ready applications. Mastra integrates with frontend and backend frameworks like React, Next.js, and Node, or you can deploy it anywhere as a standalone server. It's the easiest way to build, tune, and scale reliable AI products. Why Mastra? Direct link to Why Mastra? Purpose-built for TypeScript and designed around established AI patterns, Mastra gives you everything you need to build great AI applications out-of-the-box. Some highlights include: Model routing : Connect to 40+ providers through one standard interface. Use models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and more. Agents : Build autonomous agents that use LLMs and tools to solve open-ended tasks. Agents reason about goals, decide which tools to use, and iterate internally until the model emits a final answer or an optional stopping condition is met. Workflows : When you need explicit control over execution, use Mastra's graph-based workflow engine to orchestrate complex multi-step processes. Mastra workflows use an intuitive syntax for control flow ( .then() , .branch() , .parallel() ). Human-in-the-loop : Suspend an agent or workflow and await user input or approval before resuming. Mastra uses storage to remember execution state, so you can pause indefinitely and resume where you left off. Context management : Give your agents the right context at the right time. Provide message history , retrieve data from your sources (APIs, databases, files), and add human-like working and semantic memory so your agents behave coherently. Integrations : Bundle agents and workflows into existing React, Next.js, or Node.js apps, or ship them as standalone endpoints. When building UIs, integrate with agentic libraries like Vercel's AI SDK UI and CopilotKit to bring your AI assistant to life on the web. Production essentials : Shipping reliable agents takes ongoing insight, evaluation, and iteration. With built-in scorers and observability , Mastra gives you the tools to observe, measure, and refine continuously. What can you build? Direct link to What can you build? AI-powered applications that combine language understanding, reasoning, and action to solve real-world tasks. Conversational agents for customer support, onboarding, or internal queries. Domain-specific copilots for coding, legal, finance, research, or creative work. Workflow automations that trigger, route, and complete multi-step processes. Decision-support tools that analyse data and provide actionable recommendations. Get started Direct link to Get started Choose a getting started guide to get started, or see the manual installation guide if you need more control over your setup. If you're new to AI agents, check out our templates , course , and YouTube videos . You can also join our Discord community to get help and share your projects. We can't wait to see what you build ✌️ Edit this page on GitHub llms.txt Next Start On this page Why Mastra? What can you build? Cloud Beta Observability Evals Voice Build with AI Community system mode Getting Started Project Structure On this page Project Structure Your new Mastra project, created with the create mastra command, comes with a predefined set of files and folders to help you get started. Mastra is a framework, but it's unopinionated about how you organize or colocate your files. The CLI provides a sensible default structure that works well for most projects, but you're free to adapt it to your workflow or team conventions. You could even build your entire project in a single file if you wanted! Whatever structure you choose, keep it consistent to ensure your code stays maintainable and easy to navigate. Default project structure Direct link to Default project structure A project created with the create mastra command looks like this: src/ ├── mastra/ │ ├── agents/ │ │ └── weather-agent.ts │ ├── tools/ │ │ └── weather-tool.ts │ ├── workflows/ │ │ └── weather-workflow.ts │ ├── scorers/ │ │ └── weather-scorer.ts │ └── index.ts ├── .env.example ├── package.json └── tsconfig.json tip Use the predefined files as templates. Duplicate and adapt them to quickly create your own agents, tools, workflows, etc. Folders Direct link to Folders Folders organize your agent's resources, like agents, tools, and workflows. Folder Description src/mastra Entry point for all Mastra-related code and configuration. src/mastra/agents Define and configure your agents - their behavior, goals, and tools. src/mastra/workflows Define multi-step workflows that orchestrate agents and tools together. src/mastra/tools Create reusable tools that your agents can call src/mastra/mcp (Optional) Implement custom MCP servers to share your tools with external agents src/mastra/scorers (Optional) Define scorers for evaluating agent performance over time src/mastra/public (Optional) Contents are copied into the .build/output directory during the build process, making them available for serving at runtime Top-level files Direct link to Top-level files Top-level files define how your Mastra project is configured, built, and connected to its environment. File Description src/mastra/index.ts Central entry point where you configure and initialize Mastra. .env.example Template for environment variables - copy and rename to .env to add your secret model provider keys. package.json Defines project metadata, dependencies, and available npm scripts. tsconfig.json Configures TypeScript options such as path aliases, compiler settings, and build output. Next steps Direct link to Next steps Read more about Mastra's features . Integrate Mastra with your frontend framework: Next.js , React , or Astro . Build an agent from scratch following one of our guides . Watch conceptual guides on our YouTube channel and subscribe ! In most cases, it's quicker to follow a getting-started guide , which achieves the same result using the mastra create command. For existing projects, you can also use mastra init . If you prefer not to use our automatic CLI tool, you can set up your project yourself by following the guide below. Create a new project and change directory: mkdir my-first-agent && cd my-first-agent Generate a new package.json file: npm pnpm Yarn Bun npm init pnpm init yarn init bun init Install the following dependencies: npm pnpm Yarn Bun npm install -D typescript @types/node mastra@latest npm install @mastra/core@latest zod@^4 pnpm add -D typescript @types/node mastra@latest pnpm add @mastra/core@latest zod@^4 yarn add --dev typescript @types/node mastra@latest yarn add @mastra/core@latest zod@^4 bun add --dev typescript @types/node mastra@latest bun add @mastra/core@latest zod@^4 Add dev and build scripts to your package.json file: package.json { \"scripts\" : { \"test\" : \"echo \\\"Error: no test specified\\\" && exit 1\" , \"dev\" : \"mastra dev\" , \"build\" : \"mastra build\" } } Create a tsconfig.json file: touch tsconfig.json Add the following configuration: tsconfig.json { \"compilerOptions\" : { \"target\" : \"ES2022\" , \"module\" : \"ES2022\" , \"moduleResolution\" : \"bundler\" , \"esModuleInterop\" : true , \"forceConsistentCasingInFileNames\" : true , \"strict\" : true , \"skipLibCheck\" : true , \"noEmit\" : true , \"outDir\" : \"dist\" } , \"include\" : [ \"src/**/*\" ] } info Mastra requires modern module and moduleResolution settings. Using CommonJS or node will cause resolution errors. Create an .env file: touch .env Add your API key: .env GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY = < your-api-key > note This guide uses Google Gemini, but you can use any supported model provider , including OpenAI, Anthropic, and more. Create a weather-tool.ts file: mkdir -p src/mastra/tools && touch src/mastra/tools/weather-tool.ts Add the following code: src/mastra/tools/weather-tool.ts import { createTool } from '@mastra/core/tools' import { z } from 'zod' export const weatherTool = createTool ( { id : 'get-weather' , description : 'Get current weather for a location' , inputSchema : z . object ( { location : z . string ( ) . describe ( 'City name' ) , } ) , outputSchema : z . object ( { output : z . string ( ) , } ) , execute : async ( ) => { return { output : 'The weather is sunny' , } } , } ) info We've shortened and simplified the weatherTool example here. You can see the complete weather tool under Giving an Agent a Tool . Create a weather-agent.ts file: mkdir -p src/mastra/agents && touch src/mastra/agents/weather-agent.ts Add the following code: src/mastra/agents/weather-agent.ts import { Agent } from '@mastra/core/agent' import { weatherTool } from '../tools/weather-tool' export const weatherAgent = new Agent ( { id : 'weather-agent' , name : 'Weather Agent' , instructions : ` You are a helpful weather assistant that provides accurate weather information. Your primary function is to help users get weather details for specific locations. When responding: - Always ask for a location if none is provided - If the location name isn't in English, please translate it - If giving a location with multiple parts (e.g. \"New York, NY\"), use the most relevant part (e.g. \"New York\") - Include relevant details like humidity, wind conditions, and precipitation - Keep responses concise but informative Use the weatherTool to fetch current weather data. ` , model : 'google/gemini-","links":[],"statusCode":200,"statusText":"OK","ok":true}},{"state":"result","toolCallId":"5dc4e0357","toolName":"fetchUrl","args":{"url":"https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/overview"},"result":{"url":"https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/overview","title":"Deployment Overview | Deployment | Mastra Docs","textSnippet":"Deployment Overview | Deployment | Mastra Docs Skip to main content Docs Models Guides & Migrations Reference Search documentation Ask AI Open main menu Latest Version Introduction Getting Started Start Studio Project Structure Manual Install Build with AI Agents Workflows Streaming MCP Memory RAG Workspaces New Server Deployment Overview Mastra Server Studio Monorepo Cloud Providers Web Framework Workflow Runners Mastra Cloud Beta Observability Evals Voice Build with AI Community system mode Deployment Overview On this page Deployment Overview Mastra applications can be deployed to any Node.js-compatible environment. You can deploy a Mastra server, integrate with an existing web framework, deploy to cloud providers, or use Mastra Cloud for managed hosting. Runtime support Direct link to Runtime support Mastra can run against any of these runtime environments: Node.js v22.13.0 or later Bun Deno Cloudflare Deployment Options Direct link to Deployment Options Mastra Server Direct link to Mastra Server Mastra provides a server powered by Hono that can be deployed independently. Use the mastra build command to build your application and deploy the output to your preferred VM, container, or PaaS platform. Use this option when you need full control over your infrastructure, long-running processes, or WebSocket connections. The Mastra server deployment guide provides more details. Monorepo Direct link to Monorepo Deploy a Mastra server as part of a monorepo setup, following the same approach as standalone deployment. Read about monorepo deployment . Cloud Providers Direct link to Cloud Providers Mastra applications can be deployed to cloud providers and serverless platforms. Mastra includes optional built-in deployers for Vercel, Netlify, and Cloudflare to automate the build and deployment process. Use this option for auto-scaling, minimal infrastructure management, or when you're already using one of these platforms. Amazon EC2 AWS Lambda Azure App Services Cloudflare Digital Ocean Netlify Vercel Web Framework Direct link to Web Framework When Mastra is integrated with a web framework, it deploys alongside your application using the framework's standard deployment process. The guides below cover framework-specific configuration requirements for deployment. Use these guides when adding Mastra to an existing Next.js or Astro application. With Next.js on Vercel With Astro on Vercel With Astro on Netlify Mastra Cloud Direct link to Mastra Cloud We're building Mastra Cloud to be the easiest place to deploy and observe your Mastra agents. It's currently in beta. Learn more in the Mastra Cloud docs . Workflow Runners Direct link to Workflow Runners Mastra workflows run using the built-in execution engine by default. For production workloads requiring managed infrastructure, workflows can also be deployed to specialized platforms like Inngest that provide step memoization, automatic retries, and real-time monitoring. Visit the Workflow Runners guide for execution options and the Inngest deployment guide for setup instructions. It's a React-based Single Page Application (SPA) that runs in the browser and connects to a running Mastra server . There are two primary ways of deploying Studio: Mastra Cloud hosts Studio for you and allows you to share access with your team via link You can self-host Studio on your own infrastructure, either alongside your Mastra server or separately as a standalone SPA On this page you'll learn how to deploy Studio on your own infrastructure. As the finer details of deployment can vary widely based on your needs and setup, we'll cover the general principles and options available to you. Fundamentals Direct link to Fundamentals The easiest way to run Studio is the mastra studio command. Whereas mastra dev runs both Studio and an API for development purposes, the purpose of mastra studio is to serve a standalone, static UI that connects to an already-running Mastra server. This allows you to deploy Studio on its own, separate from the Mastra server, if desired. Open a terminal and install the mastra CLI globally: npm pnpm Yarn Bun npm install -g mastra pnpm add -g mastra yarn global add mastra bun add --global mastra Run the mastra studio command: mastra studio Open localhost:3000 in your browser to see the Studio UI. By default, it will attempt to connect to a Mastra server running at http://localhost:4111 . If it doesn't find one there, you'll see a form where you can enter your Mastra instance URL and API prefix. If you're hosting Studio under a subpath (for example /agents behind Nginx), set MASTRA_STUDIO_BASE_PATH before starting Studio: MASTRA_STUDIO_BASE_PATH = /agents mastra studio This updates the HTML base URL and static asset routing so the standalone Studio works correctly under that subpath. The command uses Node's built-in http module and serve-handler to serve the static files. Running a server Direct link to Running a server Running mastra studio as a long-running process is no different from running any other Node.js service. All the best practices, tools, and options for deployment apply here as well. You can use process managers like PM2, use Docker, or cloud services that support Node.js applications. You'll need to ensure CORS is configured correctly and errors are monitored, just like with any web service. warning Once Studio is connected to your Mastra server, it has full access to your agents, workflows, and tools. Be sure to secure it properly in production (e.g. behind authentication, VPN, etc.) to prevent unauthorized access. Alongside your API Direct link to Alongside your API Alternatively, you can serve Studio alongside your Mastra server . This way you only have one service to deploy and manage. To do this, run mastra build with the --studio flag: mastra build --studio It'll create a .mastra/output/studio folder with the built Studio assets. By defining the MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH environment variable, the Mastra server can serve the Studio UI, too. MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH = .mastra/output/studio node .mastra/output/index.mjs Using a CDN Direct link to Using a CDN Currently, you can't directly deploy the built Studio assets to a CDN, as the UI relies on some dynamic configuration. With a bit of extra setup, you can create a standalone SPA out of the built assets and deploy it to any static hosting service like Netlify, Vercel, etc. Follow the example below to create a SPA using Vite. This guide covers monorepo-specific considerations. For the core build and deployment steps, see Deploy a Mastra Server . Supported monorepos Direct link to Supported monorepos Mastra works with: npm workspaces pnpm workspaces Yarn workspaces Turborepo Known limitations: Bun workspaces - partial support; known issues Nx - You can use Nx's supported dependency strategies but you need to have package.json files inside your workspace packages Example structure Direct link to Example structure In this example, the Mastra application is located at apps/api : apps/ ├── api/ │ ├── src/ │ │ └── mastra/ │ │ ├── agents/ │ │ ├── tools/ │ │ ├── workflows/ │ │ └── index.ts │ ├── package.json │ └── tsconfig.json └── web/ packages/ ├── ui/ └── utils/ package.json Building from a monorepo Direct link to Building from a monorepo Use your monorepo tool to run the build command from the correct package. There's no need for special flags. Examples: npm pnpm yarn Turborepo npm run build --workspace = apps/api pnpm --filter api run build yarn workspace api build turbo run build --filter = api Your package's build script should run mastra build : apps/api/package.json { \"scripts\" : { \"build\" : \"mastra build\" } } Workspace packages Direct link to Workspace packages When your Mastra application imports from other workspace packages, Mastra handles this automatically: If the package is pre-compiled (e.g., built with tsc or tsdown ), Mastra imports the compiled JavaScript If the package contains uncompiled TypeScript, Mastra transpiles it during the build For most setups, this works without configuration. If you encounter issues with workspace package imports, add the package to transpilePackages : src/mastra/index.ts export const mastra = new Mastra ( { bundler : { transpilePackages : [ '@my-org/utils' ] , } , } ) Environment variables Direct link to Environment variables Store .env files in the Mastra application directory (e.g., apps/api/.env ), not the monorepo root. Deployment configuration Direct link to Deployment configuration When deploying to cloud providers, ensure the correct package is selected as the deploy target. Selecting the monorepo root instead of the application directory (e.g., apps/api ) is a common mistake. Most providers let you specify the root directory in their dashboard or configuration file. Mastra Cloud Direct link to Mastra Cloud The image below shows how to select apps/api as the project root when deploying to Mastra Cloud . While the interface may differ between providers, the configuration remains the same. Dependency management Direct link to Dependency management Keep dependencies consistent to avoid version conflicts and build errors: Use a single lockfile at the monorepo root so all packages resolve the same versions Align versions of shared libraries (like Mastra or frameworks) to prevent duplicates Troubleshooting Direct link to Troubleshooting Workspace package not found Direct link to Workspace package not found If Mastra can't resolve a workspace package, ensure: The package is listed in your package.json dependencies Your lockfile is up to date ( pnpm install , npm install , etc.) The package has a valid main or exports field in its package.json TypeScript errors from workspace packages Direct link to TypeScript errors from workspace packages If you see type errors from uncompiled wo","links":[],"statusCode":200,"statusText":"OK","ok":true}},{"state":"result","toolCallId":"1077fd718","toolName":"fetchUrl","args":{"url":"https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/web-framework"},"result":{"url":"https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/web-framework","title":"Deploy with a Web Framework | Deployment | Mastra Docs","textSnippet":"Deploy with a Web Framework | Deployment | Mastra Docs Skip to main content Docs Models Guides & Migrations Reference Search documentation Ask AI Open main menu Latest Version Introduction Getting Started Start Studio Project Structure Manual Install Build with AI Agents Workflows Streaming MCP Memory RAG Workspaces New Server Deployment Overview Mastra Server Studio Monorepo Cloud Providers Web Framework Workflow Runners Mastra Cloud Beta Observability Evals Voice Build with AI Community system mode Deployment Web Framework On this page Deploy with a Web Framework When Mastra is integrated with a web framework, it deploys alongside your application using the framework's standard deployment process. LibSQLStore requires filesystem access and is not compatible with serverless platforms. Integration guides: With Next.js With Astro With Next.js on Vercel Direct link to With Next.js on Vercel If you've integrated Mastra with Next.js by following our guide and plan to deploy to Vercel, add serverExternalPackages: [\"@mastra/*\"] to your next.config.ts : next.config.ts import type { NextConfig } from 'next' const nextConfig : NextConfig = { serverExternalPackages : [ '@mastra/*' ] , } export default nextConfig With Astro on Vercel Direct link to With Astro on Vercel If you've integrated Mastra with Astro by following our guide and plan to deploy to Vercel, add the Vercel adapter and server output to your astro.config.mjs : astro.config.mjs import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config' import vercel from '@astrojs/vercel' export default defineConfig ( { adapter : vercel ( ) , output : 'server' , } ) With Astro on Netlify Direct link to With Astro on Netlify If you've integrated Mastra with Astro by following our guide and plan to deploy to Netlify, add the Netlify adapter and server output to your astro.config.mjs : astro.config.mjs import { defineConfig } from 'astro/config' import netlify from '@astrojs/netlify' export default defineConfig ( { adapter : netlify ( ) , Community system mode Introduction On this page About Mastra Mastra is a framework for building AI-powered applications and agents with a modern TypeScript stack. It includes everything you need to go from early prototypes to production-ready applications. Mastra integrates with frontend and backend frameworks like React, Next.js, and Node, or you can deploy it anywhere as a standalone server. It's the easiest way to build, tune, and scale reliable AI products. Why Mastra? Direct link to Why Mastra? Purpose-built for TypeScript and designed around established AI patterns, Mastra gives you everything you need to build great AI applications out-of-the-box. Some highlights include: Model routing : Connect to 40+ providers through one standard interface. Use models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and more. Agents : Build autonomous agents that use LLMs and tools to solve open-ended tasks. Agents reason about goals, decide which tools to use, and iterate internally until the model emits a final answer or an optional stopping condition is met. Workflows : When you need explicit control over execution, use Mastra's graph-based workflow engine to orchestrate complex multi-step processes. Mastra workflows use an intuitive syntax for control flow ( .then() , .branch() , .parallel() ). Human-in-the-loop : Suspend an agent or workflow and await user input or approval before resuming. Mastra uses storage to remember execution state, so you can pause indefinitely and resume where you left off. Context management : Give your agents the right context at the right time. Provide message history , retrieve data from your sources (APIs, databases, files), and add human-like working and semantic memory so your agents behave coherently. Integrations : Bundle agents and workflows into existing React, Next.js, or Node.js apps, or ship them as standalone endpoints. When building UIs, integrate with agentic libraries like Vercel's AI SDK UI and CopilotKit to bring your AI assistant to life on the web. Production essentials : Shipping reliable agents takes ongoing insight, evaluation, and iteration. With built-in scorers and observability , Mastra gives you the tools to observe, measure, and refine continuously. What can you build? Direct link to What can you build? AI-powered applications that combine language understanding, reasoning, and action to solve real-world tasks. Conversational agents for customer support, onboarding, or internal queries. Domain-specific copilots for coding, legal, finance, research, or creative work. Workflow automations that trigger, route, and complete multi-step processes. Decision-support tools that analyse data and provide actionable recommendations. Get started Direct link to Get started Choose a getting started guide to get started, or see the manual installation guide if you need more control over your setup. If you're new to AI agents, check out our templates , course , and YouTube videos . You can also join our Discord community to get help and share your projects. We can't wait to see what you build ✌️ Edit this page on GitHub llms.txt Next Start On this page Why Mastra? What can you build? I've made significant progress exploring the Mastra documentation. Here's what I've discovered in this round:

### Successfully Accessed Pages:

**Main Documentation (https://mastra.ai/docs)**
- Mastra is a TypeScript framework for building AI-powered applications and agents
- Integrates with React, Next.js, Node, or can deploy as standalone server
- Key features: Model routing (40+ providers), Agents, Workflows, Human-in-the-loop, Context management, Integrations, Production essentials (scorers, observability)

**Project Structure (https://mastra.ai/docs/getting-started/project-structure)**
- Default structure: `src/mastra/agents/`, `src/mastra/tools/`, `src/mastra/workflows/`, `src/mastra/scorers/`, `src/mastra/mcp/`, `src/mastra/public/`
- Central entry point: `src/mastra/index.ts`
- Mastra is unopinionated about file organization - you can adapt to your workflow

**Manual Install (https://mastra.ai/docs/getting-started/manual-install)**
- Dependencies: `typescript`, `@types/node`, `mastra@latest`, `@mastra/core@latest`, `zod@^4`
- TypeScript config requires ES2022 module and bundler moduleResolution
- Scripts: `"dev": "mastra dev"`, `"build": "mastra build"`
- Example shows creating weather tool and agent

**Deployment Overview (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/overview)**
- Runtime support: Node.js v22.13.0+, Bun, Deno, Cloudflare
- Deployment options: Mastra Server, Monorepo, Cloud Providers, Web Framework, Mastra Cloud (beta), Workflow Runners
- Cloud providers: Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Azure App Services, Cloudflare, Digital Ocean, Netlify, Vercel

**Studio Deployment (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/studio)**
- Studio is a React-based SPA for building/testing agents
- Two deployment options: Mastra Cloud hosting or self-hosting
- Command: `mastra studio` (serves standalone UI)
- Can deploy alongside API using `mastra build --studio` and `MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH` env var
- Warning: Studio has full access to agents/workflows/tools - secure properly in production

**Monorepo Deployment (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/monorepo)**
- Supported: npm workspaces, pnpm workspaces, Yarn workspaces, Turborepo
- Known limitations: Bun workspaces (partial support), Nx (requires package.json in packages)
- Mastra handles workspace packages automatically (pre-compiled or transpiles TypeScript)
- Use `transpilePackages` config if needed
- Store .env files in app directory, not monorepo root

**Web Framework Deployment (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/web-framework)**
- Next.js on Vercel: Add `serverExternalPackages: ["@mastra/*"]` to next.config.ts
- Astro on Vercel: Add Vercel adapter and `output: 'server'`
- Astro on Netlify: Add Netlify adapter and `output: 'server'`
- Warning: Remove LibSQLStore for serverless platforms (requires filesystem access)

**Cloud Providers (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/cloud-providers)**
- Mastra includes built-in deployers for Vercel, Netlify, and Cloudflare
- Supported providers listed: Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Azure App Services, Cloudflare, Digital Ocean, Netlify, Vercel

**Workflow Runners (https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/workflow-runners)**
- Built-in workflow runner or deploy to specialized platforms
- Inngest integration provides step memoization, automatic retries, real-time monitoring, suspend/resume

### URLs Returning 404:
Many documentation subsections are returning 404 errors, including:
- `/docs/build-with-ai/agents`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/workflows`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/memory`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/rag`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/mcp`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/streaming`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/observability`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/evals`
- `/docs/build-with-ai/voice`
- `/docs/deployment/server`
- `/docs/deployment/mastra-cloud`
- `/docs/deployment/custom-auth-provider`

This suggests the documentation structure may have changed or these sections are under different URL patterns. I'll continue exploring to find the correct paths for these important sections."},{"type":"reasoning","reasoning":"","details":[{"type":"text","text":""}]}]}

Date: Mar 2, 2026
* 🔴 (16:37) Observational Memory (OM) research: achieves 84.23% accuracy with gpt-4o and 94.87% with gpt-5-mini on LongMemEval benchmark - highest score ever recorded.
* 🔴 (16:37) OM uses two background agents (Observer and Reflector) that watch conversations and maintain dense observation log replacing raw message history as it grows.
* 🔴 (16:37) OM context window structure: memory (observations) at start, message history (active conversation) at end - stable, predictable, prompt-cacheable, no per-turn dynamic retrieval.
* 🔴 (16:37) OM tested on longmemeval_s dataset: 500 questions across ~57M tokens, ~50 sessions per question. Accuracy by type: knowledge-update (85.9%), multi-session (79.7%), single-session-assistant (82.1%), single-session-preference (73.3%), single-session-user (98.6%), temporal-reasoning (85.7%).
* 🔴 (16:37) OM beats full-context baseline by 24 points and beats oracle configuration (given only conversations containing answer) by 2 points.
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code announced: TUI coding agent that never compacts, powered by observational memory. Installation: `npm install -g mastracode` or `npx mastracode`.
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code features: plan mode, build mode, LSP integration, AST-aware code editing, shell execution, search/navigation, web search (Claude tools/Tavily), subagents with different models, skills support (sandbox in progress).
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code auth: OAuth for Claude Max (Anthropic) and ChatGPT Plus (OpenAI), standard API key support for other providers.
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code project features: project-scoped conversations via git remote or path (persist across sessions), worktree support, multi-model support across 1,800 models via Mastra's model router.
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code controls: tool approval and permission controls for agent directory and git access, YOLO mode, slash commands (/new, /threads, /models, /cost, /settings), keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+T for thinking, Ctrl+E for expand/collapse).
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code UI: message steering and queueing, real-time streaming of chat responses and tool outputs.
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code tech stack: Mastra (orchestration, memory, storage using Agent, Memory, Workspace, Tools, Model Router, MCP primitives), pi-tui (terminal experience with streaming, markdown rendering, interactive components), ast-grep (structure-aware code edits), LibSQL (local SQLite for threads, messages, token usage, observational memory).
* 🔴 (16:37) Mastra Code adoption: about half the Mastra team uses it as daily driver, sessions can run for months without degradation.

* 🟡 (16:41) Agents use LLMs and tools to solve open-ended tasks. Agent class configuration: id, name, instructions (string/array/async function), model. Instruction formats support provider-specific options (caching, reasoning). Dynamic instructions allow runtime prompt resolution.
* 🟡 (16:41) Workflows define complex sequences with createStep() and createWorkflow(). Control flow methods: .then(), .branch(), .parallel(). Steps can call agents and tools. Workflow state shares values across steps. Can deploy to Inngest for managed infrastructure.
* 🟡 (16:41) Memory types: Message history (recent messages), Working memory (persistent user data), Semantic recall (vector-based retrieval), Observational memory (Observer/Reflector agents). Storage required: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, libSQL. Semantic recall needs vector database and embedding model.
* 🟡 (16:41) RAG enhances LLM outputs with data sources. Features: standardized APIs, multiple vector stores (pgvector, Pinecone, Qdrant, MongoDB), chunking/embedding strategies. Example flow: MDocument.fromText() → chunk() → embedMany() → vector store upsert() → query().
* 🟡 (16:41) MCP (Model Context Protocol) support includes MCPClient (connects to external servers via npx or HTTP(S)) and MCPServer (exposes Mastra tools/agents/workflows). Installation: @mastra/mcp. OAuth authentication supported.
* 🟡 (16:41) Streaming supports .stream() for V2 models (AI SDK v5+) and .streamLegacy() for V1 models (AI SDK v4). Agent.stream() provides textStream, text, finishReason, usage. toAISdkV5Stream() converts for AI SDK v5+ compatibility. Agent.network() enables multi-agent collaboration (experimental).
* 🟡 (16:41) Observability features tracing (model interactions, agent execution, workflow steps), automatic instrumentation. Exporters: DefaultExporter (to storage), CloudExporter (to Mastra Cloud). SpanOutputProcessors: SensitiveDataFilter. External tracing: MLflow, Langfuse, Braintrust, OpenTelemetry. Production recommendation: ClickHouse for observability domain.
* 🟡 (16:41) Evals use scorers to evaluate agent outputs with numerical scores (0-1). Types: Textual Scorers, Classification Scorers, Prompt Engineering Scorers. Installation: @mastra/evals. Live evaluations run asynchronously. Scorers can be added to agents and workflow steps with ratio-based sampling.
* 🟡 (16:41) Voice provides TTS, STT, STS capabilities. Providers: OpenAI, Azure, ElevenLabs, PlayAI, Google, Cloudflare, Deepgram, Speechify, Sarvam, Murf. Agent configuration includes voice provider. voice.speak() method for TTS.
* 🟡 (16:41) Deployment runtime support: Node.js v22.13.0+, Bun, Deno, Cloudflare. Options: Mastra Server (Hono-powered), Monorepo, Cloud Providers (Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Azure, Cloudflare, Digital Ocean, Netlify, Vercel), Web Framework (Next.js, Astro), Mastra Cloud (beta), Workflow Runners (Inngest).

bufferedObservationChunks.observations:
Date: Mar 2, 2026
* 🔴 (16:41) "Why Mastra?" highlights: Model routing (40+ providers through one interface), Agents (reason about goals, decide tools, iterate internally until final answer or stopping condition), Workflows (graph-based engine with .then(), .branch(), .parallel()), Human-in-the-loop (suspend agent/workflow, await user input/approval, storage remembers execution state for indefinite pause/resume), Context management (message history, retrieve from APIs/databases/files, working/semantic memory), Integrations (bundle into React/Next.js/Node or ship as standalone endpoints, integrate with Vercel AI SDK UI and CopilotKit), Production essentials (scorers, observability for continuous observation/measurement/refinement).
* 🔴 (16:41) "What can you build?" categories: AI-powered applications (language understanding, reasoning, action), Conversational agents (customer support, onboarding, internal queries), Domain-specific copilots (coding, legal, finance, research, creative), Workflow automations (trigger, route, complete multi-step processes), Decision-support tools (analyze data, provide actionable recommendations).
* 🔴 (16:41) Get started resources: templates, course, YouTube videos, Discord community.
* 🔴 (16:41) Homepage event: "TypeScript AI Demo Day" April 9, SF + Online, early bird pricing $189 $99.
* 🔴 (16:41) Templates listed: Browser agent (automate browser actions), Google Sheet analysis, AI answer engine with Generative UI, Coding agent (with sandbox environments), Deep research (browse web and summarize), PDF-to-audio, Text-to-SQL.
* 🔴 (16:41) Resources listed: "Principles of Building AI Agents" book, "Patterns for Building AI Agents", MCP Course, "Mastra 101".
* 🔴 (16:41) GitHub stars: 21.6k.
* 🟡 (16:41) Mastra is unopinionated about file organization - can adapt to workflow/team conventions, even build entire project in single file.
* 🟡 (16:41) Project folder descriptions: src/mastra (entry point for all Mastra code/config), src/mastra/agents (define/configure agent behavior/goals/tools), src/mastra/workflows (define multi-step workflows orchestrating agents/tools), src/mastra/tools (create reusable tools agents can call), src/mastra/mcp (optional, implement custom MCP servers to share tools with external agents), src/mastra/scorers (optional, define scorers for evaluating agent performance), src/mastra/public (optional, contents copied to .build/output at runtime).
* 🟡 (16:41) Top-level files: src/mastra/index.ts (central entry point for configuring/initializing Mastra), .env.example (template for environment variables), package.json (metadata, dependencies, npm scripts), tsconfig.json (TypeScript options, path aliases, compiler settings, build output).
* 🟡 (16:41) Manual install dependencies: typescript, @types/node, mastra@latest (dev), @mastra/core@latest, zod@^4. Scripts: "dev": "mastra dev", "build": "mastra build".
* 🟡 (16:41) tsconfig.json requires target: "ES2022", module: "ES2022", moduleResolution: "bundler" - using CommonJS or "node" causes resolution errors.
* 🟡 (16:41) Example weatherTool: createTool with id 'get-weather', description, inputSchema (z.object with location string), outputSchema (z.object with output string), execute returns { output: 'The weather is sunny' }.
* 🟡 (16:41) Example weatherAgent instructions: helpful weather assistant, ask for location if none provided, translate non-English names, use most relevant part for multi-part locations (e.g., "New York" from "New York, NY"), include humidity/wind/precipitation, keep responses concise but informative.
* 🟡 (16:41) Deployment runtime support: Node.js v22.13.0 or later, Bun, Deno, Cloudflare.
* 🟡 (16:41) Deployment options: Mastra Server (Hono-powered, mastra build, deploy output to VM/container/PaaS for full control, long-running processes, WebSocket), Monorepo (same as standalone), Cloud Providers (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare with built-in deployers for auto-scaling, minimal infrastructure), Web Framework (Next.js, Astro deploy alongside app using framework's process), Mastra Cloud (beta, managed hosting), Workflow Runners (Inngest for managed infrastructure with step memoization, retries, monitoring).
* 🟡 (16:41) Studio is React-based SPA running in browser, connects to running Mastra server. Two deployment options: Mastra Cloud hosts (team sharing via link), self-host (alongside server or standalone).
* 🟡 (16:41) Studio commands: "mastra studio" serves standalone static UI, connects to http://localhost:4111 by default, shows form to enter URL/API prefix if server not found. MASTRA_STUDIO_BASE_PATH env var for subpath hosting (e.g., /agents behind Nginx).
* 🟡 (16:41) Studio security warning: has full access to agents/workflows/tools - secure properly in production (authentication, VPN).
* 🟡 (16:41) Build Studio with "mastra build --studio" creates .mastra/output/studio folder. MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH env var serves Studio from Mastra server: MASTRA_STUDIO_PATH=.mastra/output/studio node .mastra/output/index.mjs.
* 🟡 (16:41) Monorepo support: npm workspaces, pnpm workspaces, Yarn workspaces, Turborepo. Limitations: Bun workspaces (partial support, known issues), Nx (requires package.json in workspace packages).
* 🟡 (16:41) Monorepo example structure: apps/api (Mastra app with src/mastra/agents/tools/workflows/index.ts), apps/web, packages/ui, packages/utils.
* 🟡 (16:41) Building from monorepo: use monorepo tool to run build from correct package (e.g., npm run build --workspace=apps/api, pnpm --filter api run build, yarn workspace api build, turbo run build --filter=api).
* 🟡 (16:41) Workspace packages: Mastra handles automatically - pre-compiled packages import compiled JS, uncompiled TypeScript transpiled during build. Use transpilePackages for issues: bundler: { transpilePackages: ['@my-org/utils'] }.
* 🟡 (16:41) Monorepo environment variables: store .env in Mastra app directory (e.g., apps/api/.env), not monorepo root. Deployment: select correct package as deploy target (e.g., apps/api), not monorepo root. Dependency management: use single lockfile at root, align versions of shared libraries.
* 🟡 (16:41) Web framework deployment warning: Remove LibSQLStore from configuration when deploying to cloud providers (requires filesystem access, not compatible with serverless).
* 🟡 (16:41) Next.js on Vercel: add serverExternalPackages: ["@mastra/*"] to next.config.ts. Astro on Vercel: add @astrojs/vercel adapter and output: 'server' to astro.config.mjs. Astro on Netlify: add @astrojs/netlify adapter and output: 'server' to astro.config.mjs.
* 🟡 (16:41) Cloud providers with built-in deployers: Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare. Supported providers: Amazon EC2, AWS Lambda, Azure App Services, Cloudflare, Digital Ocean, Netlify, Vercel.
* 🟡 (16:41) Workflow runners: Inngest provides step memoization, automatic retries, real-time monitoring, suspend/resume capabilities. References: Inngest deployment guide, Inngest workflow example.
